sentences.text: Yeah.
So-- I went-- I stayed there overnight.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the next, sort of, sleeping place I had at the chef's place, which I told you previously.
So all right.
We-- Let me just get into the right track.
Yeah.
So anyhow, finally we decided that we're going back to our own place where we lived originally.
Right?
Because that was-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Our furniture, everything was there, which was 56 [INAUDIBLE].
So we went there.
And the flat was occupied.
It was-- I think a director of some chemical factory.
Hungarian, yes.
I mean-- he must have got it for some reason.
And-- so he received us quite friendly and-- and nicely, but there was not much he can do because he had to live somewhere else as well.
He was-- everything.
Yes.
So I think there was enough room for us so that-- to sleep, the three of us.
But my mother had to go somewhere-- to another-- in the same house, yes, because she needed room on her own.
And somebody offered her a room on her own.
But the problem was, with Mother, really that she still was sort of very strict on her diet, kosher, and everything.
We-- we had something to eat.
He offered us, but we wouldn't touch anything.
So then, somehow we told her.
At least it was a cold winter there.
And to warm up.
I said, maybe you have a little bit of a boiled wine.
So I think she-- I don't think she had more than one sip, and she collapsed.
She fall asleep.
So we had to take her off to the place where-- where she stayed.
And we put her to bed.
But the-- every day is different.
So we woke up next day and then we started to organize ourselves some thing that has to be done.
We asked that the fellow who was occupying our flat to stay-- to-- to leave.
But he-- he needed time.
So anyhow, we just shared whatever we had.
And Mother could get hand of some food which she could really enjoy.
And the next thing was just waiting for Father and Alex to come home.
I mean, you know, it was really-- never left our-- our mind.
I mean, Mother said three times a day, when is your father coming home?
When is [INAUDIBLE] coming home?
So we started to organize.
And eventually, we got the-- we got that flat.
And we still had some finances left, so it wasn't so-- so difficult.
But we had to start to build our life anew.
I mean, we were just hoping that Father will be back.
And [INAUDIBLE] will be back in time.
Never thought for a moment that they won't be.
Still, then.
I mean.
Every day, we went to a-- there was a Red Cross center in Budapest where always new [? and new ?] names come up, you know, of people sort of who are alive and are on their way to Budapest.
And each time, when there was-- there was no sign of any of-- nothing even from my-- from my brother.
So it take--
Yes, always.
We-- all of us.
[INAUDIBLE] we settled in our flat, which we-- which we had previously.
And about two months after the liberation, or three months, we saw someone, you know, coming in the street.
And Edith was down there to buy something.
And it was my brother was coming home.
No hair.
About 36 or 38 kilo, huge man like him.
And-- and Edith didn't recognize my brother, but my brother recognized Edith.
So he came home.
So he took her up.
And you can imagine that-- the-- the laughs and the cries and the expression of emotions, every emotion in human beings.
So my mother took-- undressed Alex and put him into a bath.
And he was full of ulcers everywhere.
You know, so she was putting feather things, you know, protect it.
So we started to feed him, to look after him.
And every day, he was growing and getting stronger and stronger and stronger.
This was-- this was another sort of burst, I suppose, from my mother's part.
[INAUDIBLE].
Pardon?
That was about-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] July, August, or something.
The war ended in May.
And he was liberated in Gunskirchen, which is not far from Mauthausen.
And then, he got typhus.
He got sick.
Even through so many things.
And he didn't know what to do, it seems.
He walked part of the way home.
You know, I mean, listen, they lost every human logics.
It's-- it's unbelievable.
He came home emotionally, mentally, completely drained.
And-- and-- and he was only alive, really.
That's all.
So anyhow, he's nice, healthy man.
He [?
I think he ?] [? possibly-- ?] he created a beautiful family.
It's-- you know, you're thinking back.
You just sometimes think that the impossible is always possible.
So.
And Father still didn't come.
So we're waiting a long, and I think, you know, sort of the hope started to fade.
And the next step was to come back to live in Czechoslovakia at that time because we don't belong to Budapest.
And whatever we have, we have at home.
So we shifted ourselves without Father.
We all went back to-- not to Fil'akovo because that was a little village.
But we went back to the next bigger town, Lucenec.
And we found some place.
There was a doctor/pediatrician who didn't come back and his house was available.
So we-- so we got-- we settled in and we started to sort of rebuild our lives there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how much details I have, but things has started sort of improve somewhat.
I think I'm going to talk about my personal things, which came out of the war.
I mean, how I'm going to continue my life after-- after all this which I lived through.
The first-- my first contact after the liberation while still in Budapest was with a fellow by the name [?
Kellerman, ?] Peter.
Kellerman, ?] Peter was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somewhat connected in the Communist sort of underground.
And-- and when-- when we met, he said, listen.
We are looking for people who can be trusted.
Because in Hungary, by looks of it, no one can trust anymore, except the Jews.
Because the Communist leaders from Russia will start to come in.
And, you know, we have to sort of recreate some sort of a start for these things.
And why-- why-- come with me to the [INAUDIBLE], which was the headquarters of that time, and join us.
We need you.
So I mean, might as well do.
So I went there.
And it was a Communist sort of headquarters.
And they give me a gun.
I said, I never shot anybody or anything in my life.
So, I mean, it was sort of a part of dressing up.
And you-- you do certain work.

So the first thing was-- was that we went to the Hungarian broadcast-- the Budapest broadcasting station and clean up the things and saw what's happening and made people do the job and are supervising it.
Then, I was involved in screening the first Russian film in Budapest, which was in Rakoczi ut.
[INAUDIBLE] picture show.
And I still remember the title of this film was [INAUDIBLE].
So this-- and it was sort of a-- told that Rakoczi, who was the head of the-- of the Communist hierarchy in Russia, [INAUDIBLE] and they're coming back to Budapest.
And they will be staying in that Communist headquarters.
So I was-- the first night of his stays, I was staying in front of the door.
I was his bodyguard.
And thank god nothing happened.
I didn't have to shoot or do anything.
So we are-- we both survived.
And I started sort of-- in my mind, I started to think.
I mean, really, what am I?
Who am I?
I needed some-- some identity.
Because in Czechoslovakia, I wasn't Czechoslovakian.
I was Jewish.
Then in Hungary, I was Jewish again, but not Hungarian.
I mean, we have to be something somewhere where we can identify our self proudly and-- and-- and-- and definitely.
And I always know that I played this role in being a Gentile and everything.
I don't-- don't want to go through this one again.
I definitely got to be somebody.
So I just mentioned to my mother, you know, this Communist episode, what I'm doing.
And they asked me to join the party.
And obviously, a very religious woman.
I mean, she took it terribly.
I mean, she said, listen, I'm losing-- there's so many things-- I don't want to lose you.
I say, no, you won't lose me.
But how can a Jewish boy who believes in God, everything, you know even to contemplate anything like that?
I mean, do you want to really kill me?
I say, Mama, I'm not killing you.
I'm not going to be a [? bodyguard. ?] So the next step was they started to-- to organize an army up in Slovakia, in Poprad.
There was a Czechoslovakian unit was established in-- in Russia that was all from people who were working in this Hungarian-- Hungarian working units.
And they been-- they have been-- sort of became as prisoners of wars.
And from there, they selected and they created an army to help to fight the German.
And the place, the headquarters they had in Poprad.
Then, they ask every able, sort of Czechoslovakian, to join them because there was still some fighting was going not far from Liptovsky Mikulas in that area.
So I said, that's it.
I mean, to be a real Czechoslovakian, I have to serve in the army.
So I would like to be Czechoslovakian.
It's a democratic society and regime.
So I did everything to get to Poprad, then to-- to sign up with the army.
So I went by foot half the way.
And I got there.
So obviously, I was accepted.
So where would I stay?
What-- what-- what my experience?
They said, he's really good officers material.
So they put me in the officer school.
I think-- if I remember, there was a few days or a week.
I'm walking on the street in Poprad, and opposite me comes a captain-- Czechoslovakian captain in a Czechoslovakian military suit, uniform.
And I recognized him.
He was Dr.
Blitz who was our GP in Fil'akovo and a friend, too.
So we met, you know, with big sort of joy.
And he asked me, who-- who is alive?
Who is not alive?
And-- and whatever I knew, I told him.
And then he asked me as a kid, because I nearly was a kid.
And for him, I was even less than a kid.
What are you doing here?
I said, I joined the Czechoslovakian army.
What do you want to do Czechoslovakian army?
I want to fight the Germans soon as possible.
I say, you must be crazy.
I mean, if you joined the army.
You go in the front to-- to-- to find the Germans.
You won't be killed by the German, but you will be killed by your own soldiers in the back.
There's such a wide anti-Semitism.
And I command you now, get home.
Now at this very moment.
And I said, I can't get home.
[INAUDIBLE] Because I'm in the-- you know, I'm still signed up.
He said, that's not a problem.
You have an answer, haven't you?
I still have it.
So he gave me a medical sort of a discharge.
[LAUGHS] For your duty [? also ?] from age 11.
Yes.
So I went home.
Then-- so I start to say to myself, I was very restless.
Because I still had-- don't know what to do with myself.
So as you know, there's some young people came and people get together.
And I met a beautiful girl in Lucenec.
Really, she was gorgeous.
Beautiful.
And I fall in love like young people do.
And we're holding hands when we went to the picture show.
And sometimes I got a big smack from the elder brother, how dare you to hold hands with my sister?
You know, this sort of-- but really, she pulled me.
I mean, very much.
And I went to Budapest.
In the meantime, coming back the girl wasn't there anymore.
And I was sure that she is-- she went to a Zionist [INAUDIBLE] into Krupina.
So I mean, that was reason enough for me to-- to go to Krupina, because she was there.
So it was a weekend, and I went there, and I met her.
And she looked at me so sadly.
And she told me, I found another boyfriend.
It was too long.
I couldn't wait for you.
So-- but I was there.
So the whole life really appealed to me.
I mean, all young people looking for the same thing, I suppose, as I do.
And there was a lot of dancing, singing, and-- and-- and, you know, all this entertainment.
And in two evenings, there was a shaliach, emissary from Israel who was holding sort of seminars.
And every word he said, just sort of-- I-- I swallow it.
Everything started to grow.
You know, I could see that, you know, something happening with me.
And really, on the end I became-- I mean, if that's all we're going to have in our-- our-- our-- our land, our army, or a state.
We can be proud Jews.
And we can express ourselves the way we want to be.
Something, you know, out of there.
Something beautiful.
Because I didn't have much chance to be connected with Zionism when I was home, because that was sort of-- the situation was different.
So then-- then I decided that I'm going to-- going to an aliyah, going to Israel.
So coming home-- actually, I was put-- Which was still Palestine?
Pardon?
Oh, yes.
It was still Palestine.
So going home and I said-- again, to the mother, I'd like to talk to you about something.
I mean, she was always expecting something from me.
And I talked to her about my strong feeling.
I think it was a passionate feeling in some way, too.
I can't-- can't live here.
I can't stay here.
I need to-- an identity.
I need to do something with myself.
I'm going aliyah.
I'm going to Israel.
And she said, what about me?
You know, the same story as was always.
I said, Mom.
Have you heard this story so many times?
If you've got to go, you got to go.
I promise you that I will never leave you in my mind.
The first opportunity which will occur, I'll have you join me.
I've got to go.
And then she said somehow that Frank, who was in a-- in another town-- in Kosice.
He was in a religious, sort of a youth aliyah group.
He wants to go, too.
I said, if you go, I think I'll let Frank go too.
But you promise that you look after him.
I said, of course I will.
So that was sort of just the part when the-- the pre-actions.
But I came home, and I had to leave about two or three hours time, the train leaves.
And nothing was packed because always usually my mother do all these things.
Mother sort of objected to do anything.
Something was in her.
So I packed.
And some-- some help I got from my mother and we packed all these things together.
And we walked to the station.
It was-- wasn't so far in Lucenec.
And she looked at me, that sadness, that pain that I saw in my mother's face.
And when the time came, I hugged my mother.
I told her I love her, which I did.
I do.
I did.
And we will meet soon.
And [INAUDIBLE] please, forgive me.
I got to go.
Because she was still holding onto me.
So I went on the step on the train.
And so really, I had to really-- practically tear myself away.
And I said to myself, I'm going.
And I saw my mother.
I don't know if she fainted or something, but it was something which is always in front of my eyeballs, you know, to leave a mother like that.
And I had a forwarding address.
And when I get-- first, we get to Bratislava to-- And there was already a letter from mother apologetically that she-- she knows that this is the right thing for me to do.
But if I don't feel-- if I don't achieve what I really sought, that she would be unhappy.
Please come home.
We always wait for you with open arms.
So that was good for me.
And I suppose it was very good for her, too.
And Frank didn't go with you?
No.
Frank left six months later than I did.
Even more than six months.
Maybe a year later.
I don't know exactly because I wasn't home then.
But--
But he-- he-- Mother approved for him to leave because he's going to join me.
I left in March 1946.
So then we started these illegal aliyah.
So going-- we passed from Bratislava to Vienna.
We passed with-- as Greek repatriation.
So we had Greek papers.
And they said to them, everybody when we passed the sort of the-- the border, you're dumb.
You can't-- nobody of you can speak because we couldn't speak Greek, of course.
So you don't speak any.
We just can't speak.
I mean, that was all prearranged because the Bricha at that time, really they do a fantastic job.
They bribed.
They did everything possible when it comes.
The-- the hardest thing was to cross from Russian zone to the American zone.
But that was-- and so we got to Vienna.
And our group was-- so in the larger group, you already have sort of a-- our own group ourselves.
There was 10 boys and 10 girls.
And we really became a very close-knit family.
Not even-- it's one for all or all for one.
It was a beautiful sort of a group together.
And as we passed Vienna-- in Vienna, we had a very funny experience.
Because that time, I don't know if you have seen that film with Orson Welles, I mean, in childhood--
I mean, it was hunger.
There was nothing to eat.
And all this blackmail and all this mafia.
It was terrible.
But, you know, we were fed and looked after at that time by Joint.
And then we got these American rations.
So we didn't feel any hunger.
We got-- we got more than we needed.
And to relive the situation in Vienna, the UNRRA, which was a United Nation organization, started to bring in food to feed the Austrian people.
So they brought them by train on wagons.
And they needed trustworthy people to handle the food, to unload and reload.
So they came into the camp where we lived, and they were looking for a few people to work with.
So I was the one.
I would like to work.
So I think four of us been engaged to unload the-- to unload this railway train.
And, I mean, pinching or stealing it was holy.
Today, they put you into jail.
But back then you were a hero.
You can't-- you can sort of steal something.
So as we were packing those trucks from the-- from the wagon, we had to say loudly [NON-ENGLISH] 1, 2, 3.
But when it comes, you always picked up somehow the-- needless to say that there was always one bag of flour more than-- than we should have.
And it was in cooperation with the driver.
So we passed a school, [INAUDIBLE], where we lived.
And they already knew that we passing there, so we just throw one bag of flour down.
And then we had a bag of flour.
And it happened with-- with a few other things.
But I think butter was the premium at that time in Vienna.
So a consignment of Austrian [INAUDIBLE] butter in round-- round tin can from United Nations organization.
And they said to-- to pinch a butter, a box of butter, which I think must have been two dozen-- it's a fortune.
Because for that time, anything which was edible-- I mean, the people were giving homes away, houses away just-- just for food.
It was such a terrible situation.
So the same thing.
We succeeded to-- to pinch one box of Australian butter.
And the butter inside the tin had no sort of printed seal.
So it just ordinary.
Just a tin.
So we made some inquiry, what can we do?
And somebody was suggesting that there is a baker, an Austrian baker somewhere.
And he's a-- sort of a-- on the black market-- black market.
He's going to buy it off you.
So we took two boys.
We took that butter over there.
And we didn't know how much.
We ask him some price.
He said a big price.
But we thought, it must be worth more.
So we said double, and he paid the double.
So something did not make sense.
So quite a lot of money.
And so coming back, we're talking with someone, you know, who was a blackmail.
We said that we got so much for butter.
Say, you crazy?
You can get 10 times as much money.
So he did with us.
I don't know how much interest in the story.
Shall I continue?
Yes.
Yeah.
So the next episode came.
Similar from Australia, there was marmalade.
It was the very same sort of shape of the tin.
And someone with a bright idea.
This is not marmalade.
This is butter.
You only have to put it in a butter box, [LAUGHS] and it will be butter.
So we went back to the-- to the same place as the butter.
And he was already waiting for us.
He outside [INAUDIBLE].
You know, it's another bonanza coming.
So we made the deal.
But we asked twice as much as before.
And he paid anything.
But that wasn't butter, but it was marmalade.
[LAUGHS]
Anyhow, the word goes out.
They started-- it was-- he must have belonged to a clique of this mafia-style of black-- black marketing.
So they started to hunt us.
And they had the-- had the Austrian police on their side.
So it was at night and the-- the school where we were was circled with Austrian police.
And the police came in.
And they're going to search everything.
What are they searching for?
They're searching for drugs.
I mean, we had nothing to do with that.
But what they did, anything which was their food, they [? all ?] [? sort of ?] collected and put it in that-- in their-- sort of their wagons which they brought.
In the meantime, some of-- a lot of the boys had a chance to get to the telephone and phoned the American military police.
So while this was going on, the American military police came and surrounded the-- the Austrian police.
So the whole thing was straightened out, that this is no drugs, nothing.
And we are not involved.
We are sort of Zionists.
And we're going to Israel.
And we're not sort of dealing in anything.
So they commanded the Austrian police, they have to put back everything to the place where it was.
Now. ?]
The next day, we got a message that we should-- better leave Vienna.
Because they will get us.
Pardon?
Because Vienna was the first point of arrival.
From there on, all the distribution went everywhere.
Because that was the place--
Because they still tried to look or organize some further passage for us.
This-- that was an illegal movement.
We had to fool the British.
Over land.
I mean, we went-- we had to-- we moved from one place to another.
Because coming to-- you see, we had to-- I mean, the Bricha was working.
I mean, we had-- against the Russian.
Russian didn't want to-- the-- the-- the English was very much against it.
And the Americans supported us.
So, you know, there was sort of a confusion.
And it has to be planned in a very sort of a proper way.
We had to leave.
So it was not quite pre-arranged, but there was some chance that they could accommodate us in Salzburg.
So then we went through by train through Linz, which was the border of the Russian zone.
And again, some sort of force and some bribery, so we got through.
And we got to Salzburg, which was a military accommodation.
[INAUDIBLE] we stayed there.
I'm just going-- so from there on, we went over the border from-- from Austria to-- to Vienna, to Freilassing.
That was very close to Berchtesgaden and-- and that area.
It's beautiful aesthetically.
And-- and-- and geologically-- it was-- I mean, you know, up in the mountains.
And there was a DP camp again, so we stayed, which was called Ainring.
And the next stop from there was go through Austria and Germany.
We went to Belgium.
Because in Belgium, there was the headquarters of-- of the Jewish brigade, which was formed by the British.
And the Jewish Brigade was really, mainly-- all of the ex-Jewish brigades was involved in this Bricha.
In Belgium, there was a castle and an estate where the Bricha-- where the Jewish brigade had previously.
And they accommodated us there.
And we stayed about seven months there.
It was quite very pleasant, yes.
Was there fore-- Obviously, have to find-- I mean, there was not--
First of all, you had to be able to get through all this-- these [? ring. ?] And second, you have to have passage through the-- across the sea, or sailing.
I mean, they didn't have-- we couldn't go with a normal, sort of a sailing arrangement.
So they had to buy first some sort of old junk and convert it to something.
And then, accrue it.
So that-- that takes, really, time.
And all to do in, sort of, without the British knowing about it.
I think they did, but-- finished?
OK.
We're--
